sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Needlestick and blood-borne virus (BBV) exposure includes percutaneous injuries (needlestick, sharp instrument), mucous membrane exposure (eyes, nose, mouth), and non-intact skin exposure to blood or blood-containing body fluids.

**HIV post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) must be started within 72 hours - ideally within 4 hours - for maximum efficacy.**

**Relevant Pathogens**
| Pathogen | Transmission Risk per Percutaneous Exposure | Key Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Hepatitis B (HBV) | ~30% (source HBeAg positive) | Vaccine-preventable; HBIG available |
| Hepatitis C (HCV) | ~3% | No PEP available; no vaccine |
| HIV | ~0.3% | PEP highly effective if started promptly |

**Exposure Types - Risk Hierarchy**
**Higher risk:**
- Hollow-bore needle (IV or IM injection needle)
- Deep puncture wound
- Visible blood on device
- Device in source patient's vein or artery
- Source patient with high HIV viral load

**Lower risk:**
- Solid needle (suture needle, lancet)
- Superficial scratch
- Small volume exposure
- Source patient with undetectable viral load (HIV on treatment)

**Body Fluids That Carry Risk**
Blood, CSF, pleural/peritoneal/pericardial fluid, semen, vaginal secretions, breast milk, amniotic fluid, synovial fluid, any fluid visibly contaminated with blood.

**Body Fluids That Do NOT Carry Significant Risk**
Urine, faeces, vomit, saliva (unless visibly blood-contaminated)

sections.title: Exposure Assessment
sections.content:
**Information to Gather Immediately**
**About the injury:**
- Time of exposure
- Type of exposure: puncture, laceration, splash to eye/mouth/skin
- Body fluid involved
- Depth of wound, volume of exposure

**About the source (if known):**
- HIV status and viral load (if known)
- HBV vaccination/status
- HCV status
- High-risk behaviours (IVDU, men who have sex with men, sex worker)
- If source is unknown: consider environment (e.g., community sharps vs clinical setting)

**About the exposed worker:**
- HBV vaccination history and immune status (anti-HBs titre if known)
- Current medications (drug interactions with PEP)
- Pregnancy (relevant to PEP drug selection)
- Renal function (relevant to PEP)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**HIV PEP - Time Window**
- Start within **4 hours** of exposure where possible
- Effective up to **72 hours** - after 72 hours PEP is not recommended
- 28-day course - must be completed
- Common regimens: tenofovir/emtricitabine (Truvada) + raltegravir or dolutegravir
- Must be prescribed by a doctor - contact RFDS

**Hepatitis B Prophylaxis**
| Worker Status | Source HBsAg Positive | Source Unknown |
|---|---|---|
| Vaccinated + immune (anti-HBs >10 IU/L) | No action needed | No action needed |
| Vaccinated, immune status unknown | Check anti-HBs; give HBIG if not immune | Check anti-HBs |
| Unvaccinated | HBIG + HBV vaccine | HBV vaccine |
| Previous HBV infection | No action needed | No action needed |

- HBIG must be given within 72 hours (ideally <24 hours)
- HBIG is available through hospitals and RFDS

**Hepatitis C**
- No PEP available
- No vaccine
- Baseline serology; follow-up testing at 6 weeks, 3 months
- Early treatment (if HCV seroconversion occurs) is highly effective

**Psychological Support**
- Needlestick injuries cause significant anxiety
- Provide reassurance about low absolute transmission rates
- Ensure worker has a follow-up plan and is not isolated

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
**Immediate First Aid (Within Seconds)**
1. **Puncture/laceration:** wash with soap and water for minimum 5 minutes; encourage gentle bleeding (do NOT squeeze or suck wound)
2. **Eye/mucous membrane splash:** irrigate copiously with water or normal saline for 15 minutes
3. **Skin splash (intact skin):** wash with soap and water; risk is very low
4. **DO NOT** use bleach, caustic agents, or disinfectants on wounds (causes tissue damage without reducing transmission risk)

**Documentation (Do Immediately - Before Anything Is Forgotten)**
- Time and circumstances of exposure
- Type of device/fluid
- Source patient details (name, record number, consent for testing if possible)
- First aid administered and time
- Complete incident report per site procedure (proc-incident-reporting)

**Baseline Serology - Exposed Worker**
- HIV antibody
- HBV sAg, sAb, cAb (core antibody)
- HCV antibody
- Drawn at time of exposure - baseline status confirmation

**Contact RFDS or On-Call Doctor Immediately**
- For HIV PEP assessment and prescription
- For HBIG assessment
- Do NOT wait for serology results before starting PEP if indicated

**Source Patient Testing (If Possible)**
- Obtain verbal consent for rapid HIV, HBV, HCV testing
- Rapid HIV test (if available) can guide PEP decision
- If source declines: treat as unknown status and manage accordingly

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
**PEP Administration**
If RFDS or doctor prescribes PEP:
- **Tenofovir/emtricitabine (Truvada) + raltegravir 400 mg BD** - common regimen
- Or single tablet regimen if available
- Take with food
- 28-day course - non-adherence significantly reduces efficacy
- Common side effects: nausea, headache, fatigue - manage symptomatically
- Drug interactions: check with prescriber

**PEP Starter Packs**
- Some major mine sites stock 72-hour HIV PEP starter packs in the medical centre
- Contact WA Sexual Health and Blood-borne Virus Program for supply information
- RFDS may carry PEP on aircraft - communicate urgency

**Follow-Up Schedule**
| Timepoint | Tests |
|---|---|
| Baseline | HIV Ab, HBV panel, HCV Ab |
| 6 weeks | HIV Ag/Ab (4th generation), HCV RNA |
| 3 months | HIV Ab, HCV Ab |
| 6 months | HIV Ab, HCV Ab (if source HCV positive) |

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Notification**
- Occupational needlestick injuries are notifiable in WA under workers compensation legislation
- Complete site incident report
- WorkSafe notification if required under site obligations

**Confidentiality**
- Needlestick exposure and BBV testing results are highly sensitive
- Manage strictly confidentially
- Worker has the right to decline testing (though this affects management decisions)

**Support Resources**
- WA Sexual Health and Blood-borne Virus Program: 1800 198 205
- AFAO HIV Info Line: 1800 800 013
- Worker Assistance Program (EAP) for psychological support

**Prevention**
- Never recap used needles
- Sharps containers at point of care
- Safety-engineered devices where available
- See proc-sharps-safety for full site procedure
